structured_case.title: Brown Contracting v. City Renovations — quantum meruit for accepted work
relief_sought: Reasonable value of $96,400 in materials and labor furnished
key_facts: Plaintiff completed framing, drywall, and finish carpentry on defendant's downtown renovation between May and September 2025.
Parties never executed a written subcontract although a draft circulated by email referenced a $96,400 lump sum.
Defendant's project manager signed daily inspection logs accepting the work without objection.
Defendant invoiced the building owner for plaintiff's scope and was paid in full but refused to remit to plaintiff.

cases.name: Brown v. City Contractors
cases.holding_summary: Where a contract is unenforceable but benefits were conferred in good faith, quantum meruit may recover the reasonable value of services.

cases.name: Hartwell v. Boone Construction
cases.holding_summary: An owner who knowingly accepts the benefit of a subcontractor's work cannot escape payment by pointing to an unsigned writing.

cases.name: Smith v. Jones Mercantile Co.
cases.holding_summary: Where a buyer repudiates before the delivery date, the seller may treat the contract as breached and recover expectation damages measured by the contract price less resale proceeds.

cases.name: Miller v. Pioneer Investments
cases.holding_summary: Fraud in the inducement requires a material misrepresentation, scienter, reasonable reliance, and resulting damages.

favorable_precedents.name: Brown v. City Contractors
favorable_precedents.holding_summary: Where a contract is unenforceable but benefits were conferred in good faith, quantum meruit may recover the reasonable value of services.

favorable_precedents.name: Hartwell v. Boone Construction
favorable_precedents.holding_summary: An owner who knowingly accepts the benefit of a subcontractor's work cannot escape payment by pointing to an unsigned writing.

predicted_outcomes.methodology: Bayesian-style estimate from CAP precedent sample plaintiff outcomes, adjusted for party role and precedent depth.

scenarios.description: Judgment for plaintiff for reasonable value at or near $96,400.
Full award plus prejudgment interest and costs.
Reduction for any disputed change-order items or set-off defenses.

predicted_outcomes.likely_judgment_summary: Judgment for plaintiff for reasonable value at or near $96,400.

win_strategies.title: Lead with Brown v. City Contractors
actions: Open the brief with the Brown rule on quantum meruit.
Match each work category to inspection logs and material invoices.
win_strategies.rationale: Brown is on-point Arkansas authority; controlling precedent reduces variance.

win_strategies.title: Prove acceptance and benefit
actions: Authenticate signed daily logs through the project manager.
Subpoena defendant's pay applications to the building owner showing inclusion of plaintiff's scope.
win_strategies.rationale: Acceptance plus benefit defeats statute-of-frauds objections in quantum meruit.

win_strategies.title: Establish reasonable value
actions: Retain a construction-cost expert to opine on prevailing labor and material rates.
Provide vendor invoices and timesheets in support.
win_strategies.rationale: Quantum meruit damages turn on reasonable value, not contract price; expert proof anchors the number.

win_strategies.title: Plead alternative breach claim
actions: Plead breach of oral subcontract in the alternative.
Identify performance and acceptance acts that satisfy the partial-performance exception.
win_strategies.rationale: Alternative theories preserve recovery if the court reaches the writing requirement.

win_strategies.title: Position for early settlement
actions: Send a demand letter attaching key logs and the Brown opinion.
Offer to mediate before discovery to capture defendant's cost-of-defense calculus.
win_strategies.rationale: Construction quantum meruit cases frequently resolve once acceptance proof is irrefutable.

favorable_judgment_tactics.detail: Move for summary judgment once acceptance and benefit proofs are authenticated.
Frame the case around a single record set: logs, invoices, and pay applications.
Request reasonable value plus interest as primary relief; reserve breach damages as alternative.
Use precedent strength to demand near-full recovery in pre-discovery mediation.

complaint_outline.content: Brown Contracting Co. v. City Renovations Inc.
Plaintiff: Brown Contracting Co.; Defendant: City Renovations Inc.
quantum meruit; unjust enrichment; alternative breach of oral subcontract
Numbered paragraphs tracing the scope of work, acceptance through daily logs, and defendant's receipt of payment from the owner.
Reasonable value of $96,400; prejudgment interest from October 1, 2025; costs of suit

proposed_exhibits.description: Email thread circulating the unsigned subcontract draft
Defendant's pay applications to building owner
Key precedent: 82 Ark. 220 (1925) Brown v. City Contractors

pre_filing_checklist: Confirm five-year statute of limitations for quasi-contract claims in Arkansas.
Preserve original signed logs in escrow.
Verify filing fee and e-filing account for Faulkner County Circuit Court.
Review formatting requirements (margins, line numbering, word limits).

steps: Open e-filing portal for Circuit Court.
Upload PDF with embedded exhibits; pay filing fee.
Serve defendant per Rule 4 / local equivalent within required period.
how_to_file.note: This system does not e-file on your behalf.

top_precedents.name: Brown v. City Contractors
top_precedents.headnotes: Where a contract is unenforceable but benefits were conferred in good faith, quantum meruit may recover the reasonable value of services.
top_precedents.snippet: Subcontractor completed work accepted by the owner; failure to satisfy the statute of frauds does not permit retention of the benefit without payment.

top_precedents.name: Hartwell v. Boone Construction
top_precedents.headnotes: An owner who knowingly accepts the benefit of a subcontractor's work cannot escape payment by pointing to an unsigned writing.
top_precedents.snippet: Acceptance of the completed scope by the general contractor and payment from the owner together compel restitution to the subcontractor.
